title: Check if a number is prime
description: Given positive integer n, determine if it is prime

examples.explanation:
Step 1: n=7, check if n<=1? 7>1, continue
Step 2: Start loop i=2 to 6
  i=2: 7%2=1 (not 0), continue
  i=3: 7%3=1 (not 0), continue
  i=4: 7%4=3 (not 0), continue
  i=5: 7%5=2 (not 0), continue
  i=6: 7%6=1 (not 0), continue
Step 3: Loop completed without finding divisors
Step 4: Return True (7 is prime)

Step 1: n=9, check if n<=1? 9>1, continue
Step 2: Start loop i=2 to 8
  i=2: 9%2=1 (not 0), continue
  i=3: 9%3=0 (found divisor!), break loop
Step 3: Return False (9 is not prime, divisible by 3)

approaches.name: Brute Force O(n)
approaches.summary: Iterate from 2 to n-1 to check if any integer divides n evenly, returning false if a divisor is found. This is straightforward but inefficient for large n.

ladders.title: Full Problem — Brute Force Prime Check
ladders.desc: Complete implementation of brute force prime checking by iterating from 2 to n-1
ladders.explanation:
The brute force approach to checking if a number is prime relies on the fundamental definition of prime numbers: a positive integer greater than 1 that has no positive divisors other than 1 and itself. The intuition is straightforward - if we can find any integer between 2 and n-1 that divides n evenly (with remainder 0), then n is composite (not prime). If no such divisor exists, n is prime. This approach directly implements the mathematical definition without optimizations, making it conceptually simple but computationally expensive for large n.

Step-by-step algorithm:
1. Handle edge cases: if n ≤ 1, return false (primes must be >1)
2. For i from 2 to n-1 (inclusive):
   a. If n % i == 0, return false (found a divisor)
3. If loop completes without finding divisors, return true

Pseudocode:
function isPrime(n):
    if n <= 1: return false
    for i = 2 to n-1:
        if n % i == 0:
            return false
    return true

Time complexity: O(n) - we perform up to n-2 division operations in worst case
Space complexity: O(1) - only using constant extra space for loop variable

Connection to higher level: This is the complete implementation of the brute force approach, building upon all the foundational concepts from L1-L5. It combines edge case handling, loop iteration, division checking, and boolean logic into a complete solution.

Common mistakes:
1. Forgetting to handle n=1 (should return false)
2. Starting loop at 1 instead of 2 (1 divides everything)
3. Including n in the loop (n divides itself, but that doesn't make it composite)
4. Not using modulo operator correctly for division check

Real code pattern (Python-style):
def is_prime(n):
    if n <= 1:
        return False
    for i in range(2, n):  # from 2 to n-1
        if n % i == 0:
            return False
    return True

examples.trace:
Step 1: n=7, check if n<=1? 7>1, continue
Step 2: Start loop i=2 to 6
  i=2: 7%2=1 (not 0), continue
  i=3: 7%3=1 (not 0), continue
  i=4: 7%4=3 (not 0), continue
  i=5: 7%5=2 (not 0), continue
  i=6: 7%6=1 (not 0), continue
Step 3: Loop completed without finding divisors
Step 4: Return True (7 is prime)

Step 1: n=9, check if n<=1? 9>1, continue
Step 2: Start loop i=2 to 8
  i=2: 9%2=1 (not 0), continue
  i=3: 9%3=0 (found divisor!), break loop
Step 3: Return False (9 is not prime, divisible by 3)

ladders.title: Key Sub-routine — Divisor Checking Loop
ladders.desc: Implement the core loop that checks for divisors from 2 to n-1
ladders.explanation:
The divisor checking loop is the heart of the brute force prime checking algorithm. The intuition is that to determine if n is prime, we need to test whether any integer between 2 and n-1 divides n evenly. If we find even one such divisor, we can immediately conclude n is composite. This early exit optimization (short-circuiting) is crucial for efficiency, even in the brute force approach. The loop systematically tests each potential divisor, using the modulo operator to check for divisibility.

Step-by-step algorithm:
1. For i from 2 to n-1 (inclusive):
   a. Calculate remainder = n % i
   b. If remainder == 0, return false immediately
2. If loop completes, return true

Pseudocode:
function checkDivisors(n):
    for i = 2 to n-1:
        if n % i == 0:
            return false
    return true

Time complexity: O(n) - worst case when n is prime, we check all n-2 numbers
Space complexity: O(1) - constant space for loop variable

Connection to L0: This sub-routine forms the main computational part of the complete prime checking function. In L0, we wrap this with edge case handling (n≤1) to create the full solution.

Common mistakes:
1. Starting loop at 1 instead of 2 (1 divides everything, would always return false)
2. Going up to n instead of n-1 (n divides itself, would never find primes)
3. Not returning immediately when divisor found (inefficient)
4. Using integer division (/) instead of modulo (%) for divisibility check

Real code pattern (Python-style):
def check_divisors(n):
    for i in range(2, n):  # from 2 to n-1
        if n % i == 0:
            return False
    return True

examples.trace:
Step 1: n=5, start loop i=2 to 4
  i=2: 5%2=1 (not 0), continue
  i=3: 5%3=2 (not 0), continue
  i=4: 5%4=1 (not 0), continue
Step 2: Loop completed without finding divisors
Step 3: Return True

Step 1: n=6, start loop i=2 to 5
  i=2: 6%2=0 (found divisor!), break loop
Step 2: Return False immediately

ladders.title: Core Logic — Divisibility Testing with Modulo
ladders.desc: Check if one number divides another evenly using the modulo operator
ladders.explanation:
The core logic of prime checking is determining whether one integer divides another evenly. The modulo operator (%) provides the perfect tool for this - it returns the remainder when dividing the first number by the second. The intuition is simple: if a divides b evenly, then b % a should equal 0. This is based on the mathematical definition of divisibility: a divides b if there exists an integer k such that b = a × k. When b % a = 0, it means the division leaves no remainder, confirming perfect divisibility.

Step-by-step algorithm:
1. Given two integers a and b (where a is potential divisor, b is number being tested)
2. Calculate remainder = b % a
3. If remainder == 0, return true (a divides b)
4. Otherwise, return false (a does not divide b)

Pseudocode:
function dividesEvenly(a, b):
    return (b % a == 0)

Time complexity: O(1) - single modulo operation
Space complexity: O(1) - constant space

Connection to L1: This divisibility check is the condition inside the loop in L1. Each iteration of the divisor checking loop uses this logic to test if the current i divides n.

Common mistakes:
1. Confusing modulo with integer division (using / instead of %)
2. Checking a % b instead of b % a (wrong order)
3. Not handling edge case when a=0 (division by zero)
4. Forgetting that modulo of negative numbers can be implementation-dependent

Real code pattern (Python-style):
def divides_evenly(divisor, number):
    return number % divisor == 0

examples.trace:
Step 1: divisor=3, number=9
Step 2: Calculate 9 % 3 = 0
Step 3: Check if 0 == 0? Yes
Step 4: Return True (3 divides 9 evenly)

Step 1: divisor=4, number=10
Step 2: Calculate 10 % 4 = 2
Step 3: Check if 2 == 0? No
Step 4: Return False (4 does not divide 10 evenly)

ladders.title: Building Block — Iterating Through a Range
ladders.desc: Loop through all integers from start to end (exclusive)
ladders.explanation:
Iterating through a range of numbers is a fundamental programming pattern needed for the brute force approach. The intuition is that to check all potential divisors, we need to systematically examine each integer in a specified range. This requires understanding loop constructs and range boundaries. In the prime checking context, we need to iterate from 2 to n-1 to test each possible divisor. The loop allows us to apply the same divisibility check to every number in this range efficiently.

Step-by-step algorithm:
1. Initialize counter i = start value
2. While i < end:
   a. Perform operation with current i
   b. Increment i by 1
3. Continue until i reaches end

Pseudocode:
function iterateRange(start, end):
    for i = start to end-1:
        # do something with i

Time complexity: O(end-start) - linear in the size of the range
Space complexity: O(1) - only the loop variable

Connection to L2: The iteration provides the sequence of numbers to test for divisibility. Each iteration applies the divisibility check from L2 to the current number.

Common mistakes:
1. Off-by-one errors (including/excluding endpoints incorrectly)
2. Modifying loop variable inside loop (can cause infinite loops)
3. Using wrong comparison operator (<= instead of <)
4. Not initializing loop variable properly

Real code pattern (Python-style):
for i in range(start, end):  # from start to end-1
    # process i

examples.trace:
Step 1: start=2, end=5
Step 2: Initialize i=2
  Process i=2
  Increment i to 3
Step 3: i=3 (<5)
  Process i=3
  Increment i to 4
Step 4: i=4 (<5)
  Process i=4
  Increment i to 5
Step 5: i=5 (not <5), loop ends
Numbers processed: 2, 3, 4

Step 1: start=1, end=4
Step 2: Initialize i=1
  Process i=1
  Increment i to 2
Step 3: i=2 (<4)
  Process i=2
  Increment i to 3
Step 4: i=3 (<4)
  Process i=3
  Increment i to 4
Step 5: i=4 (not <4), loop ends
Numbers processed: 1, 2, 3

ladders.title: Basic Operation — Modulo Arithmetic
ladders.desc: Calculate remainder when dividing one integer by another
ladders.explanation:
The modulo operation is a fundamental arithmetic operation that returns the remainder of division. The intuition comes from elementary division: when we divide a by b, we get a quotient and a remainder. The modulo operator (%) gives us just the remainder. For example, 7 ÷ 3 = 2 remainder 1, so 7 % 3 = 1. This operation is crucial for checking divisibility because if a % b = 0, then a is divisible by b with no remainder. Understanding modulo is essential for many algorithms beyond prime checking, including hashing, cryptography, and circular buffer implementations.

Step-by-step algorithm:
1. Given two integers a and b (b ≠ 0)
2. Find the largest integer q such that b × q ≤ a
3. Calculate remainder = a - (b × q)
4. Return remainder

Pseudocode:
function modulo(a, b):
    return a % b  # built-in operator

Time complexity: O(1) for most implementations
Space complexity: O(1)

Connection to L3: The modulo operation is used inside the iteration loop to check divisibility of each number in the range.

Common mistakes:
1. Using modulo with zero as divisor (division by zero error)
2. Confusing with integer division (// in Python)
3. Not understanding negative number behavior (varies by language)
4. Expecting floating-point results (modulo works with integers)

Real code pattern (Python-style):
remainder = a % b

examples.trace:
Step 1: a=10, b=3
Step 2: Find largest q where 3×q ≤ 10: q=3 (3×3=9)
Step 3: Calculate remainder = 10 - (3×3) = 10 - 9 = 1
Step 4: Return 1

Step 1: a=15, b=5
Step 2: Find largest q where 5×q ≤ 15: q=3 (5×3=15)
Step 3: Calculate remainder = 15 - (5×3) = 15 - 15 = 0
Step 4: Return 0

ladders.title: Concept Foundation — Integer Comparison and Boolean Logic
ladders.desc: Compare integers and use boolean values to make decisions
ladders.explanation:
At the most foundational level, prime checking relies on comparing numbers and making decisions based on those comparisons. The intuition is simple: we need to check if numbers are equal, less than, or greater than other numbers, and then take different actions based on the results. Boolean logic (true/false values) allows us to represent these comparisons and combine them to make decisions. For prime checking, we need to compare n with 1 (to handle edge cases), compare loop indices with bounds, and check if remainders equal 0. This foundation is essential for all programming and algorithm design.

Step-by-step algorithm:
1. Compare two integers using operators: == (equal), != (not equal), < (less than), > (greater than), <= (less or equal), >= (greater or equal)
2. Get boolean result (true or false)
3. Use boolean in conditional statements to control program flow

Pseudocode:
# Basic comparisons
result1 = (a == b)  # equality check
result2 = (a < b)   # less than check
result3 = (a > b)   # greater than check

# Using in conditionals
if condition:
    # do something
else:
    # do something else

Time complexity: O(1) for single comparisons
Space complexity: O(1)

Connection to L4: Boolean results from comparisons are used to decide when to apply the modulo operation and how to interpret its results.

Common mistakes:
1. Using = (assignment) instead of == (comparison)
2. Confusing logical AND (&&) with bitwise AND (&)
3. Not understanding operator precedence
4. Forgetting that comparisons return boolean values, not integers

Real code pattern (Python-style):
is_equal = (a == b)
is_less = (a < b)
is_greater = (a > b)
if condition:
    # true branch
else:
    # false branch

examples.trace:
Step 1: Compare 5 == 3? 5 equals 3? No → False
Step 2: Compare 5 < 3? 5 less than 3? No → False
Step 3: Compare 5 > 3? 5 greater than 3? Yes → True
Output: False (equal), False (less than), True (greater than)

Step 1: Compare 7 == 7? 7 equals 7? Yes → True
Step 2: Compare 7 < 7? 7 less than 7? No → False
Step 3: Compare 7 > 7? 7 greater than 7? No → False
Output: True (equal), False (less than), False (greater than)

approaches.name: Optimized Divisor Check O(sqrt(n))
approaches.summary: Check divisors only up to the square root of n, since any factor larger than sqrt(n) must pair with a smaller factor. This reduces the number of iterations significantly.

ladders.title: Full Problem — Check if a Number is Prime Using Optimized Divisor Check
ladders.desc: Complete implementation of prime checking with sqrt(n) optimization
ladders.explanation: This is the complete solution to determine if a positive integer n is prime using the optimized divisor check approach. A prime number is defined as a natural number greater than 1 that has no positive divisors other than 1 and itself. The naive approach would check all numbers from 2 to n-1, but we can optimize significantly by only checking up to the square root of n. The intuition behind this optimization is mathematical: if n has a divisor d greater than sqrt(n), then it must have a corresponding divisor n/d that is less than sqrt(n). Therefore, if no divisor exists up to sqrt(n), none exist beyond it either. The algorithm handles edge cases: numbers less than 2 are not prime, even numbers greater than 2 are not prime (except 2 itself), and we can skip even divisors after checking 2. The time complexity is O(√n) because we iterate up to sqrt(n), and space complexity is O(1) as we use only constant extra space. This connects directly to L1 by implementing the complete prime checking logic that uses the divisor checking subroutine. Common mistakes include: forgetting to handle n=1 (not prime), not optimizing with sqrt(n), checking up to n/2 instead of sqrt(n) (less efficient), and not handling even numbers efficiently. The real pattern involves careful boundary conditions and mathematical optimization.

examples.trace:
Step 1: n=17, check if n<2? 17≥2 → continue
Step 2: check if n==2? 17≠2 → continue
Step 3: check if n%2==0? 17%2=1≠0 → continue
Step 4: compute sqrt_n = floor(√17) = 4
Step 5: iterate i from 3 to 4 (step 2):
  i=3: 17%3=2≠0 → continue
  i=5: exceeds sqrt_n → stop
Step 6: no divisors found → return True (prime)

Step 1: n=25, check if n<2? 25≥2 → continue
Step 2: check if n==2? 25≠2 → continue
Step 3: check if n%2==0? 25%2=1≠0 → continue
Step 4: compute sqrt_n = floor(√25) = 5
Step 5: iterate i from 3 to 5 (step 2):
  i=3: 25%3=1≠0 → continue
  i=5: 25%5=0 → divisor found
Step 6: divisor found at i=5 → return False (not prime)

ladders.title: Key Sub-routine — Check for Divisors Up to Square Root
ladders.desc: Determine if n has any divisor between 2 and sqrt(n)
ladders.explanation: This sub-routine is the core of the prime checking algorithm. Given a number n (assumed to be ≥2 and odd after initial checks), it checks whether n has any divisor in the range [3, floor(√n)], stepping by 2 to skip even numbers. The intuition is mathematical: if n is composite (not prime), it must have at least one divisor d ≤ √n. This is because if both divisors were > √n, their product would exceed n. By checking only up to √n, we reduce iterations from O(n) to O(√n). The algorithm computes sqrt_n = floor(√n), then loops i from 3 to sqrt_n with step 2. For each i, if n%i==0, it returns False (divisor found). If the loop completes without finding divisors, it returns True. Time complexity is O(√n) for the loop, space is O(1). This feeds into L0 by providing the divisor checking logic after edge cases are handled. Common mistakes: using floating-point sqrt without floor (precision issues), checking i≤sqrt_n instead of i≤sqrt_n (off-by-one), not stepping by 2 (inefficient for odd n), and not handling the case when sqrt_n<3. The real pattern involves integer square root computation and optimized iteration.

examples.trace:
Step 1: n=15, compute sqrt_n = floor(√15)=3
Step 2: iterate i from 3 to 3 (step 2):
  i=3: 15%3=0 → divisor found
Step 3: return False (has divisor)

Step 1: n=19, compute sqrt_n = floor(√19)=4
Step 2: iterate i from 3 to 4 (step 2):
  i=3: 19%3=1≠0 → continue
  i=5: exceeds sqrt_n → stop
Step 3: no divisors found → return True

ladders.title: Core Logic — Square Root Optimization for Divisor Checking
ladders.desc: Understand why checking divisors only up to sqrt(n) is sufficient
ladders.explanation: This level focuses on the mathematical insight that allows us to optimize divisor checking from O(n) to O(√n). The core logic is: if n is composite (not prime), it can be expressed as n = a × b, where a and b are positive integers greater than 1. Without loss of generality, assume a ≤ b. Then a ≤ √n because if a > √n, then b ≥ a > √n, making a × b > n, which contradicts n = a × b. Therefore, at least one divisor (a) must be ≤ √n. This means we only need to check potential divisors up to √n. If no divisor is found in that range, n must be prime. The intuition is about factor pairing: every divisor d > √n pairs with a divisor n/d < √n, so checking the smaller range captures all possibilities. This logic feeds into L1 by justifying the loop bound. Time analysis: checking up to √n instead of n reduces operations exponentially. Common mistakes: misunderstanding the proof, thinking √n is approximate (use floor), or applying this to n=1 or n=2 incorrectly. The real pattern involves mathematical reasoning about factors.

examples.trace:
Step 1: n=36, √36=6
Step 2: Possible factor pairs: (2,18), (3,12), (4,9), (6,6)
Step 3: In each pair, at least one factor ≤6 (2,3,4,6)
Step 4: Therefore, checking divisors ≤6 is sufficient to find if 36 is composite

Step 1: n=17, √17≈4.12, floor=4
Step 2: Check divisors 2,3,4: none divide 17
Step 3: Since no divisor ≤4 exists, by the logic, no divisor >4 exists either
Step 4: Therefore, 17 is prime

ladders.title: Building Block — Compute Integer Square Root
ladders.explanation: This intermediate concept involves computing the integer square root of n, i.e., the largest integer k such that k² ≤ n. This is needed for the sqrt(n) bound in divisor checking. Using floating-point sqrt() can cause precision issues for large n, so we use integer-based methods. The intuition: we need a precise integer bound for the loop. One simple method is to use integer arithmetic: sqrt_n = int(n**0.5) in Python, which works for moderate n but may have floating-point limits. A more robust method is iterative: start with a guess and adjust, but for this problem, the built-in sqrt is acceptable with floor. The algorithm: compute sqrt_n = floor(√n) using math.isqrt(n) in Python 3.8+ or int(math.sqrt(n)). Time complexity is O(1) or O(log n) depending on implementation, space is O(1). This feeds into L2 by providing the exact bound for divisor checking. Common mistakes: not using floor (ceil would be incorrect), floating-point rounding errors for large n, and not handling n=0 or negative n (but our problem has n>0). The real pattern involves safe integer square root computation.

examples.trace:
Step 1: n=20
Step 2: Compute √20≈4.472
Step 3: floor(4.472)=4
Step 4: Verify: 4²=16≤20, 5²=25>20 → correct

Step 1: n=25
Step 2: Compute √25=5
Step 3: floor(5)=5
Step 4: Verify: 5²=25≤25, 6²=36>25 → correct

ladders.title: Basic Operation — Modulo Operation for Divisibility Check
ladders.desc: Use n%i==0 to check if i divides n evenly
ladders.explanation: This fundamental operation checks whether an integer i divides another integer n without remainder. The modulo operator % returns the remainder when n is divided by i. If n%i==0, then i is a divisor of n (n is divisible by i). The intuition: divisibility is key to prime checking—a prime number has no divisors other than 1 and itself. The operation is computationally cheap (O(1) time) and is used repeatedly in the divisor checking loop. The algorithm: given n and i, compute remainder = n % i; if remainder==0, return True (divisible), else False. This feeds into L3 by being the core check within the square root loop. Common mistakes: confusing % with / (division), not handling i=0 (division by zero), and misunderstanding that n%1==0 always (but 1 is not checked in prime algorithm). The real pattern involves using % in conditionals for divisibility testing.

examples.trace:
Step 1: Compute 10 % 2
Step 2: 10 divided by 2 = 5 remainder 0
Step 3: remainder=0 → True (10 is divisible by 2)

Step 1: Compute 10 % 3
Step 2: 10 divided by 3 = 3 remainder 1
Step 3: remainder=1 ≠0 → False (10 not divisible by 3)

ladders.title: Concept Foundation — What is a Prime Number?
ladders.desc: Define prime numbers and basic properties
ladders.explanation: This is the simplest prerequisite concept: understanding what a prime number is. A prime number is a natural number greater than 1 that has exactly two distinct positive divisors: 1 and itself. For example, 2, 3, 5, 7 are prime. Numbers greater than 1 that are not prime are called composite (e.g., 4, 6, 8). The number 1 is neither prime nor composite by definition. The intuition: primes are the building blocks of integers—every integer greater than 1 can be factored uniquely into primes (Fundamental Theorem of Arithmetic). This concept feeds into L4 by establishing why we check divisibility. Key properties: 2 is the only even prime; all other primes are odd. There are infinitely many primes. Time/space analysis is not applicable here as it's a definition. Common mistakes: thinking 1 is prime, or that even numbers can be prime (except 2). The real pattern involves memorizing the definition and small examples.

examples.trace:
Step 1: Check if number >1? 5>1 → yes
Step 2: List divisors: 1,5
Step 3: Exactly two divisors → prime

Step 1: Check if number >1? 6>1 → yes
Step 2: List divisors: 1,2,3,6
Step 3: More than two divisors → composite

approaches.name: Sieve of Eratosthenes O(n log log n) for precomputation
approaches.summary: Precompute primes up to n using the Sieve of Eratosthenes, then check if n is prime in O(1) time. This is efficient for multiple queries on numbers up to a limit.
approaches.timeComplexity: O(n log log n) for precomputation, O(1) per query

ladders.title: Full Problem — Prime Check with Sieve Precomputation
ladders.desc: Given a positive integer n, determine if it's prime using Sieve of Eratosthenes precomputation.
ladders.explanation: This solution implements the complete Sieve of Eratosthenes approach to check if a number is prime. The intuition is to precompute all prime numbers up to n using the sieve algorithm, then simply look up whether n is prime in O(1) time. This is particularly efficient when you need to check multiple numbers up to a certain limit. The algorithm works by starting with a boolean array where all numbers are initially marked as prime. We then iterate from 2 to √n, and for each prime number found, mark all its multiples as composite. The key insight is that any composite number must have a prime factor ≤ √n, so we only need to check up to that point. After building the sieve, checking if n is prime is just accessing the array at index n. The time complexity is O(n log log n) for precomputation and O(1) per query, with O(n) space. This connects to L1 by using the sieve generation as the key subroutine. Common mistakes include not handling n=1 correctly (not prime), off-by-one errors in array indexing, and not optimizing by only checking up to √n. Real code pattern: def is_prime_sieve(n): if n < 2: return False; sieve = [True]*(n+1); sieve[0]=sieve[1]=False; for i in range(2, int(n**0.5)+1): if sieve[i]: for j in range(i*i, n+1, i): sieve[j]=False; return sieve[n]

examples.trace: Step 1: n=17 ≥ 2, proceed. Step 2: Create sieve array of size 18 (0-17), all True. Set sieve[0]=False, sieve[1]=False. Step 3: Iterate i from 2 to √17≈4. i=2: sieve[2]=True, mark multiples: 4,6,8,10,12,14,16 as False. i=3: sieve[3]=True, mark multiples: 6,9,12,15 as False. i=4: sieve[4]=False (already marked), skip. Step 4: Check sieve[17]=True, return True.

Step 1: n=20 ≥ 2, proceed. Step 2: Create sieve array of size 21 (0-20), all True. Set sieve[0]=False, sieve[1]=False. Step 3: Iterate i from 2 to √20≈4. i=2: sieve[2]=True, mark multiples: 4,6,8,10,12,14,16,18,20 as False. i=3: sieve[3]=True, mark multiples: 6,9,12,15,18 as False. i=4: sieve[4]=False (already marked), skip. Step 4: Check sieve[20]=False, return False.

ladders.title: Key Sub-routine — Generate Sieve Array
ladders.desc: Implement the Sieve of Eratosthenes to generate a boolean array marking primes up to limit n.
ladders.explanation: This sub-routine generates the sieve array, which is the core of the prime checking approach. The intuition is that we can efficiently find all primes up to n by eliminating multiples of each prime starting from 2. The algorithm initializes an array where all numbers are presumed prime, then systematically marks composites. Starting from 2 (the first prime), we mark all multiples of 2 as composite. Then move to the next unmarked number (3), mark its multiples, and continue up to √n. The key insight is that any composite number ≤ n must have a prime factor ≤ √n, so we only need to iterate up to that point. This reduces the time complexity to O(n log log n) from a naive O(n²). The space complexity is O(n) for the boolean array. This feeds into L0 by providing the precomputed array for O(1) prime checks. Common mistakes include starting the inner loop from i*2 instead of i*i (optimization), not handling the √n bound correctly, and forgetting to mark 0 and 1 as non-prime. Real code pattern: def generate_sieve(n): sieve = [True]*(n+1); sieve[0]=sieve[1]=False; for i in range(2, int(n**0.5)+1): if sieve[i]: for j in range(i*i, n+1, i): sieve[j]=False; return sieve

examples.output: [False, False, True, True, False, True, False, True, False, False, False]
examples.trace: Step 1: Create array size 11, all True. Set index 0=False, 1=False. Step 2: Iterate i=2 to √10≈3. i=2: sieve[2]=True, mark multiples 4,6,8,10 as False. i=3: sieve[3]=True, mark multiples 6,9 as False. Step 3: Result: indices 0-10: F,F,T,T,F,T,F,T,F,F,F

examples.output: [False, False, True, True, False, True, False, True, False, False, False, True, False, True, False, False]
examples.trace: Step 1: Create array size 16, all True. Set index 0=False, 1=False. Step 2: Iterate i=2 to √15≈3. i=2: sieve[2]=True, mark multiples 4,6,8,10,12,14 as False. i=3: sieve[3]=True, mark multiples 6,9,12,15 as False. Step 3: Result: indices 0-15: F,F,T,T,F,T,F,T,F,F,F,T,F,T,F,F

testCases.expected: [False, False, True, True, False, True, False, True]
[False, False, True, True, False, True, False, True, False, False, False, True, False]
[False, False, True, True, False, True, False]
[False, False, True, True, False, True, False, True, False]
[False, False, True, True, False, True, False, True, False, False]
[False, False, True, True, False, True, False, True, False, False, False, True]

ladders.title: Core Logic — Mark Multiples of Primes
ladders.desc: Given a prime number p and limit n, mark all multiples of p as composite in an array.
ladders.explanation: This core logic implements the marking step of the Sieve algorithm. The intuition is that if p is prime, then all numbers of the form k×p (where k≥2) are composite because they have p as a factor. By marking these multiples, we efficiently eliminate non-prime numbers. The algorithm takes a boolean array (initially all True), a prime p, and limit n, then sets array[k×p] = False for all k from 2 up to floor(n/p). An optimization starts from p×p instead of 2×p, because smaller multiples (like 2×p, 3×p, ..., (p-1)×p) would have already been marked by smaller primes. This reduces redundant work. The time complexity for marking multiples of one prime is O(n/p), and overall for all primes up to √n it sums to O(n log log n). This feeds into L1 as the inner loop of the sieve generation. Common mistakes include starting the loop from p instead of p×p (less efficient), incorrect loop bounds causing index errors, and not checking if p is actually prime before marking. Real code pattern: def mark_multiples(sieve, p, n): for i in range(p*p, n+1, p): sieve[i] = False

examples.input: p=2, n=10, initial sieve all True
examples.trace: Step 1: Start from p*p=4. Step 2: Iterate i=4,6,8,10. Step 3: Set sieve[4]=False, sieve[6]=False, sieve[8]=False, sieve[10]=False. Other indices remain True.

examples.input: p=3, n=15, initial sieve all True
examples.trace: Step 1: Start from p*p=9. Step 2: Iterate i=9,12,15. Step 3: Set sieve[9]=False, sieve[12]=False, sieve[15]=False. Note: 6 was already marked by p=2.

ladders.title: Building Block — Iterate and Check Up to Square Root
ladders.desc: Given a number n, iterate from 2 to √n and check conditions.
ladders.explanation: This building block involves iterating through numbers up to the square root of n, which is a common optimization in prime-related algorithms. The intuition comes from number theory: if n is composite, it must have a factor ≤ √n. Proof: if n=a×b with a≤b, then a² ≤ a×b = n, so a ≤ √n. Therefore, to check if n is prime, we only need to test divisibility by numbers up to √n, not up to n-1. This reduces the time complexity from O(n) to O(√n). The algorithm simply loops i from 2 to floor(√n), and for each i, checks if n is divisible by i. If any divisor is found, n is composite; otherwise, it's prime. This feeds into L2 as the outer loop control for the sieve, where we only need to process primes up to √n. Common mistakes include using floating-point sqrt which can cause precision errors (use integer sqrt), off-by-one errors in the loop range, and not handling n<2 separately. Real code pattern: import math; limit = int(math.sqrt(n)); for i in range(2, limit+1): # check condition

examples.trace: Step 1: Compute √25=5. Step 2: Loop i from 2 to 5 inclusive. Step 3: Check divisibility: 25%2=1, 25%3=1, 25%4=1, 25%5=0 → composite.
Step 1: Compute √17≈4.12, floor=4. Step 2: Loop i from 2 to 4 inclusive. Step 3: Check divisibility: 17%2=1, 17%3=2, 17%4=1 → no divisors found, prime.

testCases.expected: Iterate i=2 to 1 (√2≈1.41, floor=1, empty range)

ladders.title: Basic Operation — Check Divisibility
ladders.desc: Given two integers a and b, check if a is divisible by b (i.e., a % b == 0).
ladders.explanation: This basic operation checks whether one number divides another evenly, which is fundamental to prime testing. The intuition is that if a % b == 0, then b is a factor of a, meaning a is divisible by b. The modulo operator % returns the remainder when a is divided by b. If the remainder is 0, division is exact with no remainder. For prime checking, we use this to test if n has any divisors other than 1 and itself. The algorithm is straightforward: compute remainder = a % b, then check if remainder == 0. Time complexity is O(1) for fixed-size integers. This feeds into L3 as the condition checked during iteration up to √n. Common mistakes include confusing % with division (/), not handling b=0 (division by zero error), and misunderstanding that a % b == 0 implies b divides a. Real code pattern: def is_divisible(a, b): return a % b == 0

examples.trace: Step 1: Compute 10 % 2. Step 2: 10 divided by 2 is 5 remainder 0. Step 3: Remainder 0 == 0, so True.
Step 1: Compute 7 % 3. Step 2: 7 divided by 3 is 2 remainder 1. Step 3: Remainder 1 != 0, so False.

ladders.title: Concept Foundation — Prime Number Definition
ladders.desc: Understand what a prime number is: a positive integer greater than 1 with exactly two distinct positive divisors: 1 and itself.
ladders.explanation: This foundation concept defines prime numbers, which is essential for the entire problem. The intuition is that prime numbers are the building blocks of integers, as every integer greater than 1 can be uniquely factored into primes (Fundamental Theorem of Arithmetic). A prime number has exactly two positive divisors: 1 and the number itself. For example, 7 is prime because its only divisors are 1 and 7. Numbers with more than two divisors are composite (e.g., 6 has divisors 1,2,3,6). The number 1 is neither prime nor composite by definition, as it has only one divisor. This definition feeds into L4 because checking divisibility helps determine if a number has divisors other than 1 and itself. Common mistakes include thinking 1 is prime, or that prime numbers must be odd (2 is prime and even), or confusing prime with other terms like 'odd'. Real code pattern: Concept only, no code—understanding that is_prime(n) should return True if n>1 and no number between 2 and n-1 divides n.

examples.trace: Step 1: n=5 > 1. Step 2: Check divisors: 1 and 5. Step 3: No other numbers between 2 and 4 divide 5 (5%2=1, 5%3=2, 5%4=1). Step 4: Exactly two divisors, so prime.
Step 1: n=6 > 1. Step 2: Check divisors: 1,2,3,6. Step 3: Has divisors 2 and 3 in addition to 1 and 6. Step 4: More than two divisors, so composite.
